coredata.dc:title: C-scheme electron transfer mechanism: An efficient ternary heterojunction photocatalyst carbon quantum dots/Bi/BiOBr with full ohmic contact
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Guan, Yuan
Wang, Shaomang
Du, Qiongdie
Wu, Mingfei
Zheng, Zhiqian
Li, Zhongyu
Yan, Shicheng
coredata.dc:description:
With a facile one-pot solvothermal method, an efficient ternary heterojunction photocatalyst carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/Bi/BiOBr is firstly prepared. Ethylene glycol (EG) is used as the solvent and carbon source for the first time. At 190 °C for 3 h, while BiOBr is synthesized, EG is employed to prepare CQDs through bottom-up method. CQDs are grafted by a large number of functional groups with reducibility, which reduce some neighboring BiO+ to metal Bi. By modifying the solvothermal temperature and time, CQDs and Bi are in-situ controllably deposited on the surface of BiOBr microspheres. Due to different Fermi levels and work functions, the interfaces of CQDs, BiOBr and Bi are connected through ohmic junctions with low contact impedance. The hot electrons from Bi with surface plasmon resonance (SPR) properties, and electrons in the CB of BiOBr flow to CQDs, forming a C-scheme electron transfer mechanism.
O2 − from CQDs and h+ in the VB of BiOBr respectively attack the sites with higher and lower electron density in methyl orange (MO) molecule, resulting in its photodegradation into small molecular products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-scheme photocatalyst
CQDs
Bi
BiOBr
Surface defects

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) BCB-1603–2103 and (b) BCB-1901–1905 samples.
High resolution XPS scans of (a) C 1 s, (b) Bi 4f, (c) O 1 s and (d) Br 3d of BCB samples.
FTIR (a, b) and Raman (c, d) spectra of BCB-1603–2103 samples.
(a) TEM image, (b) corresponding size distribution diagram, (c) and (d) HRTEM images of CQDs.(e) HRTEM, (f) TEM, (g) EDS and (h)SAEDimagesofBCB-1903sample.

(a) UV–vis DRS spectra of CQDs solution from BCB-1603–2103. The insets show the color changes of CQDs solution illuminated by visible-light (top) and UV light (bottom). (b) UV–vis DRS spectra of BCB-1603–2103 powder suspension. (c) UV–vis DRS spectra and (d) the band-gap energies of BCB-1603–2103 samples.
(a) and (b) PL spectra of EG solution of CQDs from BCB-1903 at different excitation wavelengths. (c) Three-dimensional graph including steady-state, time-resolved PL spectra and PL quantum yield.
(a) Photocurrent, (b) Photovoltage and (c) Electrochemical impedance measurements of BCB-1603, 1903, 1905 and 2103 samples.

ESR spectra of (a) photo-generated electrons, (b)
OH radicals, (c)
O2 − radicals and (d) OVs produced by BCB-1903 in the dark and under light irradiation. (e) Photocatalytic performance of BCB-1903 for MO degradation in the absence and presence of various radical scavengers under light irradiation for 150 min. (f) MO removal profiles over BCB samples under light irradiation.
(a and b) The work functions of BiOBr (112) and Bi (012). (c) The CB/VB levels and EF of BiOBr, the LUMO/HOMO levels and EF of CQDs, and EF of Bi.
Charge transfer and built-in electric field of (a) CQDs/BiOBr, (b) BiOBr/Bi and (c) CQDs/Bi.
C-scheme electron transfer mechanism of CQDs/Bi/BiOBr.
Schematic illustration of the proposed preparation process for BCB Samples.

head.ce:title: C-scheme electron transfer mechanism: An efficient ternary heterojunction photocatalyst carbon quantum dots/Bi/BiOBr with full ohmic contact

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Eco-Materials and Renewable Energy Research Center (ERERC), College of Engineering and Applied Sciences, Nanjing University, No. 22 Hankou Road, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210093, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Eco-Materials and Renewable Energy Research Center (ERERC)
College of Engineering and Applied Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Eco-Materials and Renewable Energy Research Center (ERERC), College of Engineering and Applied Sciences, Nanjing University, No. 22 Hankou Road, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210093, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
With a facile one-pot solvothermal method, an efficient ternary heterojunction photocatalyst carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/Bi/BiOBr is firstly prepared. Ethylene glycol (EG) is used as the solvent and carbon source for the first time. At 190 °C for 3 h, while BiOBr is synthesized, EG is employed to prepare CQDs through bottom-up method. CQDs are grafted by a large number of functional groups with reducibility, which reduce some neighboring BiO+ to metal Bi. By modifying the solvothermal temperature and time, CQDs and Bi are in-situ controllably deposited on the surface of BiOBr microspheres. Due to different Fermi levels and work functions, the interfaces of CQDs, BiOBr and Bi are connected through ohmic junctions with low contact impedance. The hot electrons from Bi with surface plasmon resonance (SPR) properties, and electrons in the CB of BiOBr flow to CQDs, forming a C-scheme electron transfer mechanism.
O2 − from CQDs and h+ in the VB of BiOBr respectively attack the sites with higher and lower electron density in methyl orange (MO) molecule, resulting in its photodegradation into small molecular products.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar energy is the most valuable alternative energy because it is naturally ample, clean, and sustainable [1,2]. Photocatalysis has been a hot spot pursued by researchers since it is expected to effectively utilize solar energy [3]. It is crucial to develop efficient semiconductor photocatalysts for adopting this technology in industrial production. It is found that the combination of several semiconductors, namely construction of heterojunction, can cause the formation of spatial potential difference among semiconductors. This difference can effectively boost carrier separation of semiconductor under light irradiation [4]. In addition, the heterojunction photocatalysts usually perform enhanced photocatalytic activity owing to broader spectrum response and higher injection efficiency of charge [5]. Therefore, how to realize the intercoupling of internal electric field and interface contact among multiple semiconductors, and the smooth separation and transmission of carriers between the bulk semiconductors and interfaces, have been research focuses about charge kinetics (i.e., charge generation, transfer and consumption) in photocatalytic field [6].

Over the past several decades, as a member of the heavy p-block elements, Bi-based photocatalysts attract wide attention due to their unique electronic and structural characteristics, such as Bi2O3 [7], Bi2VO4 [8], Bi2MoO6 [9], BiOX (X = F, Cl, Br, I) [10], etc. Among them, layered BiOBr receives great interest with good ability to capture photons and inherent electrostatic field to promote separation of electron-hole pairs [11–13]. To advance carriers separation of BiOBr, various modification methods were put into application, such as impurity doping [14], heterojunction formation [15,16], crystal facet engineering [17], vacancy defect construction [18] and surface metallization [19].

As a spherical nano carbon materials with size of less than 10 nm, CQDs have unique photoluminescence (PL) properties, including the excitation wavelength-dependent as well as down- and up-converted emissions, which result in its effective utilization of the full spectrum of sunlight [20]. As an excellent electron donor and acceptor [21,22], CQDs are grafted by a large number of functional groups, which can provide abundant surface defects to trap and release electrons quickly [23]. Therefore, the preparation and application of CQDs based composites have become a research hotspot in recent years. Among them, the CQDs/semiconductor composites have been widely studied in the fields of photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants [24,25], photocatalytic oxidation [26] and photocatalytic overall water splitting [27], etc. Besides, due to the strong plasmonic properties of noble metal nanoparticles (e.g., Au [28] and Ag [29]), noble metal/semiconductor heterojunctions have enhanced photoactivity resulting from two main factors: Schottky junctions and localized SPR [30]. For non-noble metal Bi nanoparticles, a large number of valence electrons are easy to migrate and become hot electrons under light irradiation, which make Bi nanoparticles also exhibited SPR properties, so as to provide more reactive species [31]. Compared to pure BiOX (X = Cl, Br), Bi/Bi2O2CO3 [31], Bi/BiOCl-Ti3C2 [32] and Bi/BiOBr [33] photocatalysts could more efficiently degrade pollutants in water under light irradiation.

Therefore, different from the previously reported multi-step solvothermal methods, we adopted one-pot method to successfully synthesize a ternary heterojunction photocatalyst CQDs/Bi/BiOBr with full ohmic contact and C-scheme electron transfer mechanism. By modifying the solvothermal temperature and time, CQDs and Bi were in-situ controllably deposited on the surface of BiOBr microspheres. At 190 °C for 3 h, EG was used as the solvent and carbon source for the first time. While BiOBr was synthesized by solvothermal method, EG was employed to fabricate CQDs through bottom-up method. The CQDs were grafted by a large number of functional groups with reducibility. When CQDs were deposited on the surface of BiOBr, some neighboring BiO+ was in-situ reduced to metallic Bi by CQDs [34]. Due to the abundant surface defect states and oxygen-enriching properties of CQDs [35], as well as SPR effect of metallic Bi, the composite photocatalyst possessed broader visible light response, higher separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers, and more excellent photocatalytic activity.
Scheme 1 illustrates the synthesis mechanism of BCB Samples. For BCB samples, the preparation, characterization, DFT calculations and photocatalysis were seen in supporting information.

Fig. 1 a and b reflect the XRD patterns of the as-synthesized samples. For all BCB samples, the diffraction peaks at 10.90, 25.21, 31.72, 32.27, 46.28 and 57.20° are indexed to (001), (101), (102), (110), (200) and (212) crystal planes of tetragonal phase BiOBr (PDF#85-0862). In BCB-1903, 1905 and 2103, the new peaks at 27.16, 37.95 and 39.62° attribute to (012), (104) and (110) facets of hexagonal phase Bi (PDF#85-1329), suggesting the formation of bismuth metal. Due to lower concentration and higher dispersion, any XRD patterns of CQDs cannot be found in BCB samples. With increasing reaction temperature and time, the peak intensity of metallic Bi increases gradually, indicating that the elevated temperature and time are beneficial for the growth of CQDs, which can reduce more BiO+ to Bi. It is worth noting that the diffraction peaks of BiOBr in all samples do not change after Bi and CQDs deposition, indicating that the metallic Bi and CQDs are present only on the surface of BiOBr.

The crystallite sizes of Bi particles are estimated using the Scherrer formula (1).
(1)
D =
0.94 λ
β cos θ
where D and λ are crystalline size and wavelength (1.54 Å) of X-ray radiation, respectively. β and θ are full width at half maximum of the diffraction peak and the diffraction angle, respectively. According to the diffraction peak of (012) facet of metal Bi, the Bi grain sizes of BCB-1903, BCB-1905 and BCB-2103 are calculated to be 60, 84 and 68 nm, respectively.

The surface elemental composition and valence state of BCB samples were comparatively studied by XPS measurements. Fig. S1 shows the full survey spectra of BCB-1603,1903,1905 and 2103 samples, which are composed of C, Bi, O and Br elements. The C 1 s peaks of BCB samples are displayed in Fig. 2 a. The C 1 s region for BCB-1603 is fitted by three peaks at 284.6, 286.1 and 288.7 eV, which are ascribed to CC/CC, COH and HOCO carbon, respectively [36]. The appearance of these peaks confirms the existence of CQDs in the sample. For BCB-1903, the intensity of CC/CC (284.6 eV) and COH (285.7 eV) peaks increases significantly, suggesting the raise of OH modified CQDs. Compared with BCB-1603, the two peaks at 285.7 and 288.1 eV migrate towards lower binding energy, resulting from the increase of valence electron density around COH and HOCO carbon. In BCB-1905 and 2103, the intensity of CC/CC peak decreases sharply, and the peaks around 288 eV disappear to be replaced by OCOC and COC peak at 286.7 and 286.4 eV, respectively. With the increase of reaction time and temperature, the hydroxyl groups on the surface of CQDs are oxidized to ester and ether groups, respectively.

The high resolution XPS spectra of Bi 4f are depicted in Fig. 2b. For BCB-1603, two strong peaks at 159.4 and 164.7 eV are attributed to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of Bi3+, which are consistent with the literature of BiOBr [37]. In BCB-1903, the above two peaks exhibit a shift of 0.3 eV toward lower binding energy, which is indicative of the formation of lower valence bismuth. An additional weak peak at 156.8 eV belongs to Bi 4f7/2 of Bi0, demonstrating that partial BiOBr has been reduced to Bi0 by CQDs. For BCB-1905, the peak at 160.1 eV is ascribed to Bi 4f7/2 of Bi3+. Compared with BCB-1603, the binding energy has a higher shift of 0.7 eV, resulting from the decrease of electron cloud density around BiO+. Besides, the stronger peak at 162.0 eV is assigned to Bi 4f7/2 of Bi0, which explains that more external BiO+ is in-situ reduced into Bi0. In BCB-2103, the two weak peaks at 159.4 and 164.7 eV belong to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of Bi3+, which originate from less BiOBr on the surface. The prominent peak at 161.8 eV suggests that Bi element mainly exists in the form of Bi0.

Fig. 2c reveals the HRXPS spectra of O 1 s electrons. For all samples, the O 1 s peaks around 530 and 532 eV belong to the BiO bonding in [Bi2O2] slabs and CO groups from CQDs, respectively. The different values of binding energies indicate that the electronegativity distinction of oxygen ion. For BCB-1903, the third O peak is ascribed to the hydroxyl oxygen (531.1 eV), confirming that the surface of its CQDs is coated with a large number of OH groups [38].

In Fig. 3 d, two peaks at 68.5 and 69.6 eV are assigned to Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2 of Br- from BiOBr in BCB-1603, respectively. Interestingly, none of any Br 3d peaks appear in BCB-1903. The results illustrate that superficial BiOBr has been reduced by CQDs, leading to overflow of Br- from its surface. With increasing of reaction time, BiOBr on the surface of BCB-1905 decomposed gradually to produce Br2, which could substitute hydrogen atom on functional groups of CQDs to complete bromination reaction. Consequently, both of Br 3d peaks at 71.7 and 72.8 eV belong to covalently attached bromine atoms. For BCB-2103, the Br 3d peaks at 69.6, 70.9 and 71.7 eV are ascribed to Br- from BiOBr, physisorbed Br2 on the surface, and covalent bonded Br atom, respectively.

The chemical structure and functional groups of BCB samples were studied by FTIR spectra. As shown in Fig. 3a and b, all of BCB samples have characteristic peaks at 516 and 878 cm−1, which are ascribed to the stretch vibrations of BiO in BiOBr [39]. A broad and strong peak at 3436 cm−1 refers to stretch vibrations of hydroxyl groups with strong hydrogen bonding of intra- and intermolecular type. The weak absorption peak at 2349 cm−1 is caused by CO2 in the air. The characteristic absorption bands at 1045, 1618 and 2852–2921 cm−1 belong to the stretch vibration of COC, CO and CH2, respectively, indicating the existence of CQDs in BCB samples. For BCB-1903, the two absorption peaks at 1393 and 2970 cm−1 are assigned to stretch vibration of CC and CH3, respectively, confirm the construction of SP2 and SP3 hybrid carbon in its CQD [40].

The surface structure of BCB samples was further analyzed via Raman spectroscopy, depicted in Fig. 3c and d. For all samples, the peaks at 90–100 cm−1 are ascribed to the A 1 g internal Bi-Br stretching mode. In BCB-1903, another band at 150 cm−1 is assigned to the E 1 g internal BiBr stretching mode [41]. Besides, there are broad bands appearing in the scope of 300–600 cm−1 in BCB-1903, which are stretching, bending, and rocking modes of BiO [42]. None of any peaks for BiO vibration are present in BCB-1603, 1901 and 1905. The weak bands of BiO vibration are also seen in BCB-2103. These results imply that the stronger interaction between Bi and O elements exists in BCB-1903. Noticeably, the characteristic bands of carbon materials at the range of 1212–1460 cm−1 only appear in BCB-1903. The Raman spectra of BCB-1903 and its CQDs are shown in Fig. S2, in which locations of their characteristic peaks are basically consistent. The CQDs structures on the surface of BCB-1903 are same as that in the EG solution. The bands at 861, 1038–1089, 1212–1460 cm−1 belong to CC stretching vibration of CCO, CO stretching vibration and SP3 hybrid carbon (CH2 and CH3) deformations, respectively. Due to the dominance of SP3 hybrid carbon in BCB-1903, the G-band of SP2 hybrid carbon is not observed [43]. The Raman results also confirm that the CQDs are grafted by a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups.

The morphologies of BCB samples were investigated by SEM (Fig. S3). BCB-1603 was self-assembled compactly by nanosheets to form hierarchical microspheres. The size of BiOBr particles is 3.6–4.3 μm. When the reaction temperature was raised to 190 °C, the microsphere size of BCB-1903 decreases to 1.5 μm. The BCB-1903 particles are formed loosely by stacking nanoflakes. When the temperature was increased to 210 °C, the hierarchical microsphere of BCB-2103 is damaged, and replaced by layered petal like structure with thinner nanosheets. It is found that the increased reaction temperature can reduce the size of BiOBr particles, but unfavorable for its growth. At 190 °C for 1 and 5 h, the microsphere sizes are 1.2 and 3.3 μm, respectively. It shows that the extension of reaction time can make BiOBr particles size obviously increased. Fig. 4 depicts the EDS elemental mapping of BCB-1903. The EDS pattern approve the existence of C, O, Br and Bi elements, which supports that CQDs/Bi/BiOBr is successfully fabricated. The elemental mapping demonstrates that C, O, Br and Bi elements are evenly distributed in BCB-1903.

As shown in Fig. 5 a, the CQDs in the upper suspension of BCB-1903 are spherical, well dispersed and free of agglomeration. From the histogram of particle size distribution (Fig. 5b), the distribution range of CQDs is 4.5–9.5 nm, and their average particle size is 6.6 nm. Fig. 5c and d exhibit that the lattice spacing of 0.320 nm corresponds to the (002) plane of CQDs with graphite phase [44]. In Fig. 5f, the microsphere diameter of BiOBr in BCB-1903 is further observed to be 1.5 μm. On the edge of the microsphere, a detailed crystal lattice was obtained by HRTEM measurement (Fig. 5e). Three kinds of clear lattice fringes with d spacing values of 0.229, 0.320 and 0.328 nm are indexed as the (112) plane of BiOBr, the (002) plane of CQDs, and the (012) plane of Bi, respectively. In addition, the EDS result (Fig. 5g) indicates that BCB-1903 contains C, O, Br and Bi elements. The higher C content means that more CQDs exist in BCB-1903. The corresponding SAED pattern (Fig. 5h) presents the coexistence of diffraction spots and rings, which illustrates that large polycrystalline particles, and small single crystal particles appear in the diffraction area. These particles own different grain orientations, resulting in the formation of diffraction ring connected by diffraction spots.

Fig. S4a and b shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of BCB-1603–2103 and 1901–1905 samples. According to the Brunauer-Deming-Deming-Teller classification, these isotherms are classified as IV type isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops, indicating the existence of mesoporous in the samples. Using the BET method, the specific surface areas (SSA) of BCB-1603, 1903, 2103, 1901 and 1905 are calculated as 25.21, 24.20, 21.60, 24.06 and 26.01 m2 g−1, respectively. The higher reaction temperature is not beneficial to the increase of SSA, while the longer reaction time can effectively enlarge SSA. The corresponding pore size distribution curves are displayed in Fig. S4c and d. The pore sizes of BCB-2103 are only situated at 2–4 nm, which explain the emergence of a large number of microporous structure. Except for BCB-2103, the pore sizes of other samples locate at 2–4 and 5–40 nm, which further confirm the formation of mesoporous structure.

As displayed in Fig. 6 a, the CQDs from BCB samples show strong absorption peaks in the UV region with a tail extending into the visible range. The absorption peaks in the range of 200–250 nm are attributed to the π - π * transition of SP2 hybrid carbon on CQDs [45]. Due to the push-pull electron effect of oxygen-containing groups, the π - π * absorption bands are split into two peaks. For BCB-1903–2103, the absorption peak in the range of 250–300 nm belongs to the n - π * transition of CO [46], which correlates with the fluorescence properties of CQDs. Compared with BCB-1903, BCB-1905 and 2103 have stronger absorption peaks, which indicate that their CQDs own more oxygen-containing groups, resulting in enhanced fluorescence intensity. For BCB-1603–2103, the colors of CQDs solution change from colorless to brownish yellow under visible-light irradiation. When the solutions of BCB-1903–2103 are illuminated by UV light irradiation (λ = 365 nm), the colors display bright blue-green fluorescence. The UV–vis DRS spectra of BCB-1603–2103 powder suspension is exhibited in Fig. 6b. Among them, the π - π * transition absorption peak intensity of BCB-1903 is stronger in the range of 200–250 nm, illustrating the higher CC content of its CQDs.

UV–vis DRS spectra of BCB-1603–2103 samples are given in Fig. 6c. The BCB samples exhibit extended absorption edges in the visible-light region, which red shift from 425 to 475 nm. As shown in inset of Fig. 6c, the samples’ colors change from light white to dark gray owing to the emergence of CQDs and increasement of Bi content. With the color transformation from light to deep, the absorption edges red shift gradually. The results explain that the formation of CQDs and Bi makes BiOBr ready for the efficient utilization of photon energy in the visible-light range.

Moreover, band-gap energy (Eg) was calculated following the equation: (αhν)1/2 = A(hν-Eg), where α, hν, Eg and A are the absorption coefficient, photon energy, band gap and constant, respectively. The Eg values of as-synthesized samples are estimated from a Tauc plots of (αhν)1/2 versus the photon energy (hν), as shown in Fig. 6d. The intercepts of the tangent to the x-axis give a good approximation of the band-gap energies for these samples. The Eg values are estimated to be 2.75 eV for BCB-1603, 2.62 eV for BCB-1901, 2.38 eV for BCB-1903, 2.25 eV for BCB-1905 and 2.13 eV for BCB-2103. With the increase of absorption band-edges, the Eg values decrease gradually.

The PL spectra of CQDs solution from BCB-1903 are shown in Fig. 7 a and b. Corresponding to the excitation wavelengths of 340 and 480 nm, the optimal emission peaks locate at 430 and 550 nm, respectively. When the excitation wavelength increases from 320 to 600 nm, the emission peak gradually redshifts. The CQDs of BCB-1903 have obvious down-conversion PL properties, and their locations of emission peaks are greatly dependent on the excitation wavelength [26].

The steady-state PL spectra of BCB samples are depicted in Fig. 7c. All the samples display an intrinsic state emission peak in the range of 450–455 nm, resulting from spontaneous radiation transition of photo-generated electrons. Due to the inclusion of slight CQDs, the PL peak of BCB- 1603 is basically equal to that of pure BiOBr. The PL peak intensity of BCB-1603 is stronger, indicating that the introduction of few CQDs does not effectively reduce the recombination of electron and hole pairs. With the increase of reaction temperature and time, the content of CQDs increases, the PL peak intensity of other BCB samples decreases, which illustrate that the introduction of moderate CQDs can advance the non-radiative transition of photo-generated electrons. Among them, the peak intensity of BCB-1903 is weaker, indicating the higher electron-hole separation efficiency.

Previous reports confirmed that intrinsic state emission (electron-hole recombination, quantum size effect and zig-zag sites) and defect state emission (surface energy traps) synchronously contributed to fluorescence of CQDs [47]. If there are many defect states exiting on the surface of CQDs, photo-generated excitons can be trapped there. The captured excitons can reduce the probability of its direct recombination, which weaken the intrinsic luminescence of exciton state. According to the structural characterization results, the surface of BCB-1903 is covered by CQDs grafted with different functional groups,such as OH, COOH, CH3. These functional groups bring about the formation of more hanging bonds on its surface, resulting in the generation of abundant surface defects [23]. As shallow defect, the surface defects can swiftly capture and release photo-generated carriers. The transient trapping not only can effectively promote the separation of electron and hole pairs, but also avoid the formation of new recombination centers due to the existence of bulk defects.

To track the carrier migration dynamics on BCB samples, the time-resolved PL spectra was used to reveal the transition modes of photo-generated electrons from excited state to ground state. For declining to ground state, the photo-generated electrons release energy through radiative transition (emitting fluorescence), and non-radiative transition (interacting with other atoms, such as collision and migration). The non-radiative transition can accelerate the transportation of electrons from excited state to ground state, leading to the increase of fluorescence decay rate and the decline of fluorescence lifetime [5]. As displayed in the time-resolved PL spectra, the fluorescence lifetime of BCB-1903 (τ = 1.11 ns) is shorter, suggesting the faster transfer of the photo-induced electrons. The defects on the surface of CQDs can capture and release electrons more quickly, so as to effectively improve the separation of carriers. The investigation implies that surface defects can not only trap electrons, but also increase the active sites of the photocatalytic reaction. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime of BCB-1905 (τ = 2.32 ns) is less than BCB-2103 (τ = 2.48 ns) and BCB-1603 (τ = 2.65 ns), which indicates that the appropriate amount of oxygen-containing functional groups on CQDs can also boost non-radiative transition of photo-generated electrons, in favor of carrier separation.

The bar graph of PL quantum yield (PLQY) was employed to demonstrate the transition modes of photo-excited electrons in BCB samples. Among them, the PLQY value of BCB-1903 (PLQY = 0.96 %) sample is lower, which indicates that the photo-generated electrons mainly release energy in the form of non-radiative transition, featuring a weaker emission peak and a shorter fluorescence lifetime. In contrast, the PLQY value of BCB-1603 (PLQY = 2.75 %) is higher, illustrating energy liberation of its electrons through radiative transition. These fluorescence analysis techniques confirm that the recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes from BiOBr is effectively inhibited due to the introduction of CQDs and metal Bi in BCB-1903.

To further examine separation efficiency of photo-generated carriers from BCB samples, the photocurrent, photovoltage and electrochemical impedance measurements were conducted. Among these samples, BCB-1903 exhibits the highest photocurrent and photovoltage, and lowest impedance (Fig. 8 a, b and c), which imply that more reactive species such as electrons and holes can be generated over BCB-1903 under light irradiation [48]. These reactive species will participate in the photocatalytic reaction, so as to enhance the photocatalytic activity of BCB-1903.

ESR measurements were conducted to verify the existence of photo-induced carriers, radical species and OVs. As a stable electron identification reagent, TEMPO can react with photo-generated electrons to produce non-paramagnetic TEMPOH, which weakens its original ESR signal [49]. In the mixed system of TEMPO and BCB-1903 powder, the intensity of ESR spectra after photo-irradiation are significantly weaker than those in the dark (Fig. 9 a), which indicates that a large number of photo-generated electrons and holes are generated from BCB-1903. The ESR signals of
OH radicals are originated from the production of DMPO/
OH. As shown in Fig. 9b, we cannot observe ESR characteristic peaks of DMPO/
OH before and after illumination, implying that the adsorbed H2O or OH− cannot be oxidized to
OH by photogenerated holes.

In the suspension including DMPO, methanol and BCB-1903, the stronger ESR characteristic peaks of DMPO/
O2 − are found under light irradiation (Fig. 9c), which result from a large number of
O2 − radicals generated in BCB-1903 suspension. As reported in the literature, when the aqueous solution containing 0.1 wt% of CQDs is exposed to air, the total oxygen content of the suspension measures double that of pure water [35]. If CQDs possess abundant photogenerated electrons under illumination, the adsorbed O2 on their surface can be reduced to a large number of
O2 − radicals. The oxygen enrichment mechanism of CQDs will benefit molecular O2 or ambient air as a sustainable oxidant, leading to extraordinarily high efficiency for photocatalytic oxidation or reduction while using CQDs modified BiOBr as photocatalysts. Additionally, the existence of OVs on the surface of photocatalyst can also promote the production of
O2 − radicals. Accordingly, solid-state ESR measurements were used to test OVs on the surface of BCB-1903. It is well acknowledged that the ESR signal at g = 2.0 is attributable to OVs generated by the well-known Zeeman effect [50]. As shown in Fig. 9d, it can be surveyed that the ESR characteristic peak intensity of OVs (g = 2.006) upon photo-irradiation is significantly stronger than that in the dark, suggesting the existence of abundant OVs.

To further clarify the roles of reactive species generated in the photocatalytic process, EDTA-2Na, BQ and IPA were used as the respective scavengers for h+,
O2 − and
OH. From Fig. 9e and S5, after 150 min of light irradiation, the degradation rate of MO over BCB-1903 is 93.38%. With the addition of EDTA-2Na, BQ and IPA, the degradation rate is decreased to 27.39%, 41.28% and 80.88%, respectively. It was clear that the introduction of EDTA-2Na and BQ evidently restrained MO degradation, while only a slight decrease was observed with IPA. This demonstrates that h+ and
O2 − are the two dominant radicals for MO degradation, with a minor contribution from
OH, which are consistent with ESR measurements.

According to theoretical calculations, the LUMO and HOMO levels of MO were about −2.36 eV (Vs NHE) and 0.82 eV (Vs NHE), respectively (Fig. S6b and c). Compared with HOMO level of MO, the reduction potential of
O2 −/O2 （-0.33 eV Vs NHE ）is lower [51], and the oxidation potential of h+ is higher. It can be predicted that the chemical bond on HOMO level of MO should be first broken by attack of
O2 − and h+. Although the 2p orbitals of 7 N, 8 N,15 N, 1C, 3–5C and 9–14C contribute to HOMO (Fig. S6a and c),
O2 − typically attack the positions with a more positive point charge under the force of electric field, and h+ on the contrary [25]. Therefore, 15 N and12C are easier to be attacked by h+ and
O2 −, respectively (Fig. S6d and e), resulting in breaking of the chemical bond between them. It should be noted that the electron density around 7 N and 8 N is high, indicating that these positions would be oxidized by h+ to cause decolorization of MO. Finally, MO is photodegraded into small molecule products under the action of
O2 − and h+.

As shown in Fig. S7, after 30 min, MO could reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium on BCB samples in the dark. Under light irradiation of 150 min, MO was hardly self-degraded (Fig. S8). Fig. 9f displayed that MO was degraded at varying degrees over BCB samples. The degradation rates of MO over BCB-1603, BCB-1901, BCB-1903, BCB-1905 and BCB-2103 were 65.96%, 73.60%, 93.38%, 77.65% and 67.93%, respectively. Among them, BCB-1903 performs the highest photocatalytic activity. The results affirm that the appropriate CQDs and metal Bi greatly improve the photocatalytic activity of pure BiOBr. The experimental data was modeled as the first-order kinetics equation (Fig. S9 and S10). The degradation rate constant (k) of MO over BCB-1903 was the greatest and nearly three times of that over BCB-1603. The k values obtained by BCB samples are arranged as the following order: BCB-1903 > BCB-1905 > BCB-1901 > BCB-2103 > BCB-1603.

The photocatalyst stability plays an important role in its application. Consequently, the cycling experiments of BCB-1903 were implemented, depicted in Fig. S11. With the increase of the cycle times, the photocatalytic activity slightly decreases. After 810 min of reaction, the degradation rate of MO dropped from 93.38% to 87.91%, indicating its favorable cycle stability. After four cycling runs, the XRD patterns of BCB-1903 are shown in Fig. S12. We can see that there are not apparent changes of its phase structure. The tetragonal phase BiOBr, hexagonal phase Bi and CQDs still coexisted in BCB-1903. After four rounds of recycling use, the SEM images and FTIR spectra of BCB-1903 are displayed in Fig. S13 and 14. It is clear that the degradation products of MO only adhere to the surface of BCB-1903 without changing its original microsphere structure and surface functional groups.

As shown in Fig. 10 a, b and Fig. S15, the work functions of BiOBr (112) and Bi (012) were 4.99 and 3.70 eV, with corresponding to the Fermi levels of 0.49 and −0.8 eV (Vs NHE), respectively. In Fig. 10c and Fig. S16, the LUMO and HOMO levels of CQDs were separately calculated to be −0.40 and 0.58 eV (Vs NHE), which indicates that the CQDs are a semiconductor material with good light absorption capability (Eg = 0.98 eV). Generally speaking, the Fermi level of n-type semiconductor is 0.2 eV lower than the conduction band (CB) edge (or 0.2 eV higher than the valence band (VB) edge for p-type semiconductor). Therefore, the Fermi level of CQDs is located in the range of −0.20 to 0.38 eV. The Bi (012) has the highest Fermi level, followed by CQDs and BiOBr (112) in sequence.

Besides, the levels of CB and VB edges of BiOBr are predicted by the following empirical equations [29].
(2)
E C
= χ -
E 0
- 0.5
E g
(3)
E V
=
E C
+
E g

Herein, EC and EV are CB and VB edge energies of the semiconductor, respectively. Eg is the band-gap energy of the semiconductor. χ is the electronegativity of the semiconductor. E0 is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (about 4.5 eV). The band-gap energy and the electronegativity of BiOBr (χ) were 2.75 and 6.17 eV, respectively. Consequently, EC and EV values of BiOBr are 0.30 and 3.05 eV (Vs NHE).

As depicted in Fig. 11 a, the Fermi level of CQDs is higher than that of BiOBr. When they come into contact under dark, to build up an equilibrium state of the Fermi level between CQDs and BiOBr, the surface electrons of CQDs diffuse to BiOBr (accumulating negative charges), whereas the holes are left on one side of CQDs (accumulating positive charges). As a result of the diffusion, an internal electric field from CQDs to BiOBr is formed at the interface of the two semiconductors. This makes the energy bands of BiOBr and CQDs bending down and upward, respectively. After illumination, electrons of BiOBr and CQDs are elevated to the CB and holes are remained in the VB. Compared with the VB position of BiOBr, CQDs has a higher HOMO level. Driven by the built-in electric field, the electrons in the CB of BiOBr are easier to recombine with the holes in the HOMO level of CQDs. Accordingly, the electrons in the CB of CQDs and the holes in the VB of BiOBr are left for photocatalytic reduction and oxidation reactions, respectively. This spatial separation of electrons from holes makes the contact between BiOBr and CQDs similar to the ohmic junction with low contact resistance [6].

Owning the highest Fermi level, the work function of metal Bi is less than that of BiOBr and CQDs, which make the two metal-semiconductor interfaces give ohmic contact with low resistance. After contact under dark (Fig. 11b and c), the electrons of metal Bi separately diffuse to BiOBr and CQDs, resulting in the upward shift of Fermi levels of BiOBr and CQDs. It should be noted that due to the high density of free electrons in metal, the electron flow can hardly alter its Fermi level [6]. When the Fermi energy levels of BiOBr and CQDs are consistent with that of metal Bi, the diffusion of electrons reaches dynamic equilibrium. An internal electric field is built, directing from the surface of semiconductors to their respective bulk phases, which prevents electrons of metal Bi from further diffusing to BiOBr and CQDs. Due to the SPR effect, the energetic hot electrons from metal Bi are injected into the CB of BiOBr and CQDs under illumination, respectively. For Bi/BiOBr junction, the injected hot electrons will flow back to the metal Bi in the absence of Schottky barrier, resulting in no enrichment of excess electrons in the CB of BiOBr. However, as an excellent electron repository, the surface of CQDs is full of defects, which prevent the hot electrons from returning to metal Bi (Fig. S16). Finally, a large number of hot electrons from metal Bi are enriched in the CB of CQDs for photocatalytic reduction reaction.

For the ternary heterojunction photocatalyst CQDs/Bi/BiOBr, both of electrons from Bi and BiOBr flow to CQDs, leading to the formation of a C-scheme electron transfer mechanism (Fig. 12 ). The electrons in the LUMO of CQDs and holes in the VB of BiOBr, are used for photocatalytic reduction and oxidation reactions, respectively.

This work employed one-pot solvothermal to construct a novel ternary heterojunction photocatalyst CQDs/Bi/BiOBr. Based on the improvement of traditional multi-step solvothermal [33,41], the CQDs and metal Bi were simultaneously deposited on the surface of BiOBr to enhance its photocatalytic performance. Compared to other BiOBr composites [28,29], the interfaces among CQDs, BiOBr and metal Bi in the composite are combined by ohmic junction with low contact impedance. Different from the current electron separation principles [52,53], a C-scheme electron transfer mechanism was proposed for the first time. The hot electrons from Bi with SPR properties, and electrons in the CB of BiOBr flow to CQDs, forming a C-scheme electron migration path. This mechanism not only shortens the migration distance of electrons, but also enables electrons smoothly transferring between bulk semiconductors and interfaces, resulting in efficient separation of carriers. In the suspension of MO containing CQDs/Bi/BiOBr powers under irradiation, the generated
O2 − from CQDs, and the h+ left on the VB of BiOBr can attack these positions with more positive and negative point charges in MO molecules, respectively. Finally, MO is photodegraded into small molecule products under the action of
O2 − and h+. This paper provides a new strategy for the application of CQDs and Bi-based semiconductors in photocatalytic environmental remediation and energy conversion.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

The characterization was supported by the teachers of analysis and testing center, NERC biomass of Changzhou University.
